event: 30 years old
African American
presented to the emergency department
complaining of shortness of breath on exertion
chest pain with deep inspiration
otherwise, the patient stated she was well
no indication of recent infections
physical examination did not yield any abnormalities or concerns
VQ scan indicated high probability for pulmonary emboli in her right lobe
chest x ray showed possible development of small bilateral pleural effusions
multiple nodular opacities scattered throughout the lungs
CT scan of the abdomen showed moderate alveolar consolidation in the right lower lobe
EKG was normal
2D echocardiograms were normal
no indication of endocarditis
admitted for treatment of an acute pulmonary embolism
Heparin drip was initiated
developed high fevers
tachycardia
blood cultures were positive
yielded Gram positive cocci in pairs and clusters indicative of Staphylococcus species
subsequent identification by Vitek 2 GP card indicated the bacterium was S. caprae
initially started on vancomycin
antibiotics were changed to Kefzol 2 gm IV every 8 hours
fever resolved
showed significant clinical improvement
urinalysis indicated the presence of E. coli
urinary tract infection
Ciprofloxacin was administered to treat the urinary tract infection
three days of Ciprofloxacin was administered
no indication that the patient recently visited a farm or had contact with goats
transplants, internal fixation for orthopedic prosthesis, or foreign subject implants were not in the patient’s medical history
patient contracted S. caprae during her hospital stay
S. caprae had been misidentified in the past
the identity of S. caprae was established by the current Vitek 2 GP system
this isolate produced acid from mannitol and was urease positive
some S. caprae strains have acquired methicillin resistance
S. caprae may play a role in conferring virulence to this species
early detection and accurate identification of this bacterium will help preventing serious complications
